original_abstract: Photonic computing offers a transformative pathway toward high-speed, energy-efficient vision processing. Despite this potential, practical deployment has been hindered by the complexities of optical nonlinearities, the heavy power demands of the analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) required for digital post-processing, and a high sensitivity to systemic noise. In this work, we introduce ACCEL, an all-analog chip that fuses electronic and light-based computing to overcome these barriers. ACCEL achieves a systemic energy efficiency of 74.8 peta-operations per second per watt (PetaOPS/W) and a processing speed of 4.6 PetaOPS—with over 99% of tasks executed optically. These metrics represent improvements of three and one orders of magnitude, respectively, over current state-of-the-art processors. By utilizing diffractive optics as an inherent feature encoder, the resulting photocurrents are processed directly within an integrated analog circuit. This architecture bypasses the need for ADCs entirely, reducing frame latency to just 72 ns. Through the joint optimization of optoelectronic hardware and adaptive training protocols, ACCEL demonstrates experimental classification accuracies of 85.5% on Fashion-MNIST, 82.0% on 3-class ImageNet, and 92.6% for time-lapse video recognition. Furthermore, the system exhibits exceptional robustness under ultra-low-light conditions (0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame). These capabilities position ACCEL as a versatile solution for power-constrained, high-throughput applications, including wearable electronics, autonomous vehicles, and industrial inspection systems.
humanized_abstract: Photonic computing is considered as a promising approach for fast, power-efficient vision processing. However, photonic computing is held back by optical nonlinearity, ADC power, and noise resilience. In this work, we design and implement an all-analog chip ACCEL (optical–electrical Accelerator for Computer vision using Light) that integrates the electronic and photonic processing units to address the challenges in photonic computing. Our design achieves a system level power efficiency of 74.8 peta-operations per second per watt (PetaOPS/W) with processing throughput of 4.6 PetaOPS while achieving over 99% optical processing with three orders of magnitude improvement in power efficiency and one order of magnitude improvement in processing throughput when compared with state-of-the-art computing systems. The photocurrent generated due to feature encoding using diffractive optics is processed within an integrated analog circuit without the need of converting them into digital signals using analog-to-digital converters. This leads to a frame latency of only 72 ns. Through joint optimization of photonic–electronic hardware and the learning algorithm, the system achieves experimental classification accuracies of 85.5% on Fashion-MNIST, 82% on 3-class ImageNet, and 92.6% on time-lapse video classification. The system is highly resilient under ultra low light conditions of 0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame. In summary, our system demonstrates the potential of ACCEL in various power-constrained, high-throughput applications including that of wearable electronics, self-driving cars, and industrial inspection systems.

document.output: Photonic computing is considered as a promising approach for fast, power-efficient vision processing. Our design achieves a system level power efficiency of 74.8 peta-operations per second per watt (PetaOPS/W) with processing throughput of 4.6 PetaOPS while achieving over 99% optical processing with three orders of magnitude improvement in power efficiency and one order of magnitude improvement in processing throughput when compared with state-of-the-art computing systems. The photocurrent generated due to feature encoding using diffractive optics is processed within an integrated analog circuit without the need of converting them into digital signals using analog-to-digital converters. This leads to a frame latency of only 72 ns. Through joint optimization of photonic–electronic hardware and the learning algorithm, the system achieves experimental classification accuracies of 85.5% on Fashion-MNIST, 82% on 3-class ImageNet, and 92.6% on time-lapse video classification. In summary, our system demonstrates the potential of ACCEL in various power-constrained, high-throughput applications including that of wearable electronics, self-driving cars, and industrial inspection systems.
